mechanism: Proposed mechanism: Garlic contains organosulfur compounds; alliin can be enzymatically converted to allicin in preparations, but specific clinically confirmed mechanisms for lipid/BP effects are not established well enough here to label as confirmed in humans.
activeCompounds: Alliin (S-allyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide) and allicin are two well-characterized organosulfur compounds commonly referenced in garlic chemistry.
safetyNotes: Oral garlic has been used safely in long-term research (up to 7 years) with common adverse effects including odor, abdominal discomfort, flatulence, nausea, and possible allergic reactions; topical raw garlic can cause chemical burns. Bleeding risk is a key safety concern for supplement forms. Pregnancy/breastfeeding safety is unclear for supra-food oral amounts.

sources.title: *Allium sativum* L. - Plants of the World Online (Kew)

description: Cultivated worldwide with long historical medicinal/culinary use; used orally as food and supplements, and sometimes topically—though raw garlic on skin can cause severe irritation/chemical burns.
dosage: No single standardized dose is established across products. Use product-labeled dosing and clinical guidance, especially when bleeding risk or polypharmacy is present.
interactions: Unresolved: interaction profile is not adequately characterized in current repository sources.
Use medication review caution, especially with CNS-active, cardiovascular, anticoagulant, and hepatically metabolized drugs.
legalStatus: Widely approved as food and supplement globally (GRAS).
preparation: Food use (fresh/cooked) plus supplement forms (varied preparations) and topical forms; raw topical use is specifically cautioned due to burn risk.
region: Native range is described as Central Asia to northeastern Iran in POWO (with global cultivation).
sideEffects: may cause bleeding in high doses (anticoagulant effect)
summary: Garlic is widely used as food and as a dietary supplement; human data suggest small reductions in total cholesterol/LDL in hypercholesterolemia, small blood-pressure reductions in hypertension, and small blood-sugar reductions in diabetes, while evidence for cancer risk reduction is mixed/uncertain. Key caution: supplement use may increase bleeding risk, especially around surgery or with anticoagulants/antiplatelets.
